perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it will take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or nonpermitted use of Confidential Information of the other party in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures will include the highest degree of care that either party utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that it will attempt to take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of Confidential Information of the other party, in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain. The specific measures employed are left to the party's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will take all reasonable measures' with 'will attempt to take reasonable measures' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. Removing the clause 'which measures will include the highest degree of care that either party utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature' eliminates a crucial benchmark for determining what is 'reasonable,' allowing for less stringent protective actions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modification weakens the 'reasonable measures' and removes the benchmark of 'highest degree of care' for protecting confidential information, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by CUTSA to maintain trade secret protection, especially in cases involving highly sensitive information.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information by replacing 'will take all reasonable measures' with 'will attempt to take reasonable measures,' and removing the clause establishing a benchmark for reasonableness. The Law Explanation states that this may not meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard, especially for sensitive information. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies California Civil Code 3426 as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The modification therefore creates an ambiguity that could lead to non-compliance, as the measures employed are left to the party's discretion, creating a contradiction under the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: Company is aware, and will advise its Representatives who are provided any Confidential Information of ZipRealty, of the restrictions imposed by federal securities laws on the purchase or sale of ZipRealty’s securities by any person who has received material non-public information from ZipRealty or its Representatives and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person may purchase or sell ZipRealty’s securities while in possession of such information. Company agrees to require its Representatives to comply with these restrictions.
perturbation.changed_text: Company is aware, and will attempt to advise its Representatives who are provided any Confidential Information of ZipRealty, of the potential restrictions relating to federal securities laws, which may apply to the purchase or sale of ZipRealty’s securities. Company will encourage its Representatives to comply with these restrictions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will advise' to 'will attempt to advise' introduces ambiguity about whether the representatives will actually be informed. Replacing 'restrictions imposed by federal securities laws' with 'potential restrictions relating to federal securities laws' weakens the clarity and force of the warning. Changing 'Company agrees to require its Representatives to comply' to 'Company will encourage its Representatives to comply' significantly weakens the mandatory compliance aspect.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The original text ensures awareness and mandated compliance to prevent insider trading. The altered text weakens these safeguards. Encouraging rather than requiring compliance, and watering down the advisory aspect increases the likelihood of insider trading violations which the original clause tried to prevent, undermining the prohibitions in Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Securities Exchange Act of 1934 The Securities and Exchange Act of 1934 ("1934 Act," or "Exchange Act") primarily regulates transactions of securities in the secondary market . As such, the 1934 Act typically governs transactions which take place between parties which are not the original issuer , such as trades that retail investors execute through brokerage companies. In contrast, the Securities Act of 1933 prior to the Exchange Act established regulations for issuers and listings on the primary market . Disclosures To protect investors, Congress crafted a mandatory disclosure process designed to force companies to disclose information that investors would find pertinent to making investment decisions. In addition, the Exchange Act regulates the exchanges on which securities are sold. Regulation FD is the primary section of the Exchange Act which discusses disclosures. Reporting Requirements The required disclosures and forms of disclosure vary depending on the situation and the registrant. In general, under Section 13(a) of the Exchange Act (codified in 15 U.S.C. § 78m ), companies with registered publicly held securities and companies of a certain size are called "reporting companies," meaning that they must make periodic disclosures by filing annual reports (called a Form 10-K ) and quarterly reports (called a Form 10-Q ). Reporting companies must also promptly disclose certain important events (called a Form 8-K ). These periodic reports include or incorporate by reference types of information that would help investors decide whether a company's security is a good investment. Information in these reports includes information about the company's officers and directors, the company's line of business, audited financial statements , and the management discussion and analysis section. The Exchange Act also mandates disclosure at certain crucial points so that investors can make an informed decision before purchasing stock. Sections 14(a)-(c) (codified in 15 U.S.C. § 78n(a)-(c) ) govern disclosure during proxy contests, when various parties might solicit an investor's vote on a corporate action, or to vote for certain board members. All disclosure materials must be filed with the SEC. Tender Offers The Securities Exchange Act requires disclosure of important information by anyone seeking to acquire more than 5 percent of a company's securities by direct purchase or tender offer . Such an offer often is extended in an effort to gain control of the company. If a party makes a tender offer, the Williams Act governs (codified as 15 U.S.C. § 78m(d)-(e) ). A tender offeror must also file disclosure documents with the SEC that disclose its future plans relating to its holdings in the company. This information allows investors to decide whether to sell or not. Securities and Exchange Commission Section 4 of the Exchange Act established the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) , which is the federal agency responsible for enforcing securities laws. SEC Required Disclosures One important function of the SEC is to ensure that
perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates that the company 'will advise' its representatives of the restrictions and 'agrees to require' compliance. The changed text weakens these obligations by stating the company 'will attempt to advise' and 'will encourage' compliance. This creates ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of the original compliance requirements. The Law Explanation states that Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibit manipulative and deceptive devices related to securities. The changed text undermines the original intent to prevent insider trading, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Unless previously approved by the Board of Directors of the disclosing party, each receiving party will not, and will instruct and use its best efforts to cause its Representatives not to, directly or indirectly: (a) inquire about, announce or make any offer or proposal (including, without limitation, any offer or proposal to the stockholders of the disclosing party) concerning an Acquisition Transaction (as defined below); (b) knowingly encourage, solicit or discuss with, or provide any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to, any person or entity with respect to any inquiry or announcement regarding or the making of any offer or proposal concerning any Acquisition Transaction; NDA -2- (c) otherwise knowingly facilitate or participate in any effort or attempt to make or implement any Acquisition Transaction; (d) participate in any “solicitation” of “proxies” to vote (as such terms are used in the rules and regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission), or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of, any securities of the disclosing party; (e) otherwise act or seek to control or influence the management, Board of Directors or other policies of the disclosing party; or (f) request the disclosing party or any of its Representatives to amend or waive any provisions of this paragraph 9.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless previously approved by the Board of Directors of the disclosing party, each receiving party will generally refrain from, and will endeavor to encourage its Representatives to avoid, directly or indirectly: (a) inquire about, announce or make any offer or proposal concerning a potential Acquisition Transaction; (b) encourage, solicit or discuss with any person or entity an inquiry or announcement regarding or the making of any offer or proposal concerning any Acquisition Transaction, as much as reasonably possible; NDA -2- (c) facilitate or participate in any effort to make or implement a potential Acquisition Transaction, to the extent practicable; (d) participate in any “solicitation” of “proxies” to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of, any securities of the disclosing party, as appropriate; (e) otherwise act or seek to control or influence the management, Board of Directors or other policies of the disclosing party, when feasible; or (f) request the disclosing party or any of its Representatives to amend or waive any provisions of this paragraph 9, unless deemed necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications weaken the restrictions on the receiving party related to acquisition transactions. Changing 'will not, and will instruct and use its best efforts to cause its Representatives not to' to 'will generally refrain from, and will endeavor to encourage its Representatives to avoid' weakens the obligation. Adding phrases like 'potential,' 'as much as reasonably possible,' 'to the extent practicable,' 'as appropriate,' 'when feasible,' and 'unless deemed necessary' introduces significant ambiguity and loopholes, making the standstill provision much less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 requires any person or group of persons who acquire beneficial ownership of more than 5% of a class of a company's equity securities to file a Schedule 13D or 13G disclosure with the SEC. The original standstill clause aimed to prevent actions that could lead to an acquisition without board approval. By weakening the restrictions, the modifications allow for actions that could circumvent the intent of Section 13(d), such as coordinated efforts to influence the company's policies or acquire shares indirectly, without triggering the reporting requirements of Section 13(d) due to the ambiguity now present in the clause. This increased risk of non-compliance with required disclosures.

Periodical and other reports (a) Reports by issuer of security; contents Every issuer of a security registered pursuant to section 78l of this title shall file with the Commission, in accordance with such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate for the proper protection of investors and to insure fair dealing in the security- (1) such information and documents (and suc
perturbation.justification: The original contract text uses strong, definitive language to prevent certain actions related to acquisition transactions. The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens these restrictions through phrases like 'generally refrain,' 'endeavor to encourage,' 'potential,' 'as much as reasonably possible,' 'to the extent practicable,' 'as appropriate,' 'when feasible,' and 'unless deemed necessary.' This ambiguity creates a contradiction because it makes the obligations less enforceable. The law explanation suggests this increased ambiguity and weakened language risks non-compliance with disclosure requirements under Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 78m, concerns periodical and other reports and mandates that issuers of securities file information with the Commission. While the snippet doesn't explicitly detail Section 13(d) or its reporting requirements, the Law Explanation suggests weakening the standstill provisions could allow actions circumventing the reporting requirements. Thus, the modifications undermine the intent to prevent undisclosed accumulation of shares, creating a contradiction. The altered language creates an ambiguous obligation, making enforcement difficult and risking non-compliance, aligning with the 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' definition.
